summary:
The 1973 oil crisis began in October 1973 when the Arab members of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OAPEC) imposed an oil embargo on nations they considered to be supporting Israel during the [[event:2687|Yom Kippur War]], primarily targeting the United States, the Netherlands, and other Western countries. The embargo lasted until March 1974 and had dramatic effects on the global economy.

The price of oil quadrupled in a matter of months, from roughly three dollars per barrel to nearly twelve dollars. The sudden shortage triggered long lines at gasoline stations in the United States and Europe, rationing, and emergency conservation measures including reduced speed limits and daylight saving time. The economic shock contributed to a severe global recession, combining inflation with stagnation — a condition economists labeled stagflation — that would characterize Western economies for much of the remainder of the decade.

The crisis exposed the deep structural vulnerability of industrialized economies to the price and supply of a commodity controlled by a small group of producing nations. It prompted major shifts in energy policy in affected countries, including increased investment in domestic oil production, nuclear power, and energy efficiency. The strategic importance of the Persian Gulf and Middle Eastern oil producers was suddenly elevated in global geopolitics.

The longer-term consequences included accelerated development of North Sea oil fields, reduced oil dependency in some sectors, and a fundamental reassessment of energy security as a dimension of national strategy. The crisis also strengthened OPEC's political leverage and demonstrated that commodity producer cartels could exercise substantial power over consuming nations.
